steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage compares two long novels: 1Q84 has 1,157 pages and ~407,000 words; A Moment in the Sun has 1,100 pages and ~625,000 words. A Moment in the Sun has fewer pages but far more words, so it must pack more words onto each page. The blank must follow directly from this math.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the edition of A Moment in the Sun has more words per page than the edition of 1Q84.: 625,000 words ÷ 1,100 pages ≈ 568 words/page vs. 407,000 ÷ 1,157 ≈ 352 words/page. A Moment in the Sun packs more words per page. This follows directly and necessarily from the data given. ✓
  • Choice B — translating a text from Japanese to English is likely to increase the word count.: The passage mentions the translation but gives no comparison between the Japanese original and the English translation. Only one data point exists for 1Q84 — we cannot conclude anything about translation effects from a single example. ✗
  • Choice C — page count is likely to be a more reliable indicator of how long it takes to read a book than word count.: The passage says nothing about reading time. The data actually suggests page count is unreliable as a proxy for length, since a 1,100-page book can contain far more words than a 1,157-page one. This is the opposite of what the data implies. ✗
  • Choice D — the edition of 1Q84 is printed on thinner paper than the edition of A Moment in the Sun.: Paper thickness is never mentioned and cannot be inferred from word counts or page counts alone. The passage provides no basis for this claim. ✗
That is the correct answer.
